title: Nucleic Acids
objectives: Describe phosphate, pyrophosphate, ATP, and nucleotide components.
Distinguish nucleosides, nucleotides, purines, pyrimidines, ribose, and deoxyribose.
Apply 5-prime to 3-prime polarity, phosphodiester bonding, complementarity, and Chargaff relationships.
Explain DNA double-helix stability, melting temperature, and helix dimensions.
Compare prokaryotic supercoiling with eukaryotic histone packaging, chromatin, centromeres, and telomeres.
Differentiate DNA and RNA chemistry and identify major coding and non-coding RNA types.

sections.title: Phosphate chemistry powers nucleotide biology
sections.content: Phosphate can form high-energy anhydride linkages, including pyrophosphate and ATP phosphoanhydride bonds. Nucleotides contain a sugar, a purine or pyrimidine base at carbon 1, and one to three phosphate groups at carbon 5.

sourceRefs.quote: Nucleotides are the building blocks of RNA and DNA. Each nucleotide contains a ribose or deoxyribose sugar, a purine or pyrimidine base joined to carbon 1 of the sugar ring, and one, two, or three phosphate units joined to carbon 5 of the sugar ring.

sections.title: DNA nucleotides combine deoxyribose, base, and phosphate
sections.content: DNA building blocks are deoxyribonucleoside 5-prime triphosphates. Deoxyribose lacks the 2-prime hydroxyl found in ribose, adenine and guanine are purines, cytosine and thymine are pyrimidines, and the sugar-phosphate portion is the invariant backbone.

sourceRefs.quote: The sugar-phosphate portion of a nucleotide is the invariant backbone of nucleic acid, while the base is the variable portion.

sections.title: Polynucleotides have direction and complementary base pairing
sections.content: Phosphodiester bonds connect the 3-prime hydroxyl of one sugar to the 5-prime phosphate of the next, so sequences are written 5-prime to 3-prime. In double-stranded DNA, antiparallel strands pair A with T and G with C.

sourceRefs.quote: In double-stranded DNA, two long polynucleotide chains hydrogen-bond in antiparallel orientation, meaning the 5-prime end of one chain is paired with the 3-prime end of the other.

sections.title: Base stacking, hydrogen bonding, and GC content stabilize DNA
sections.content: The Watson-Crick model describes a right-handed double helix with bases inside and the sugar-phosphate backbone outside. GC-rich DNA has a higher melting temperature than AT-rich DNA because GC pairs have three hydrogen bonds while AT pairs have two.

sourceRefs.quote: AT-rich DNA has a lower Tm than GC-rich DNA because AT pairs have two hydrogen bonds while GC pairs have three.

sections.title: Genomes are compacted by supercoils or chromatin
sections.content: Prokaryotic circular chromosomes are compacted by DNA gyrase-mediated supercoils. Eukaryotic DNA wraps around histone octamers to form nucleosomes and chromatin; heterochromatin is denser and less accessible, while euchromatin is looser and more transcriptionally active.

sourceRefs.quote: DNA wrapped around histone octamers forms nucleosomes, giving a beads-on-a-string appearance before complete packing.

sections.title: RNA is less stable and has coding and non-coding forms
sections.content: RNA is usually single-stranded, uses uracil instead of thymine, and contains ribose with a 2-prime hydroxyl that can attack the backbone phosphate. mRNA carries coding information, while tRNA, rRNA, snRNA, miRNA, siRNA, and long non-coding RNAs perform non-coding functions.

sourceRefs.quote: RNA differs chemically from DNA in three important ways: RNA is single-stranded except in some viruses, RNA contains uracil instead of thymine, and RNA contains ribose rather than 2-prime deoxyribose.

equations.expression: A = T; G = C; A + G = T + C
equations.meaning: Chargaff relationships follow from complementary purine-pyrimidine pairing in double-stranded DNA.

sourceRefs.quote: Chargaff's rule states that A equals T, G equals C, and A plus G equals T plus C.

equations.expression: GC pair = 3 hydrogen bonds; AT pair = 2 hydrogen bonds
equations.meaning: GC-rich DNA requires more thermal energy to melt than AT-rich DNA.

sourceRefs.quote: The GC pair has three hydrogen bonds, while the AT pair has two.

equations.expression: DNA helix turn = 34 angstroms = 10 base pairs
equations.meaning: The Watson-Crick helix repeats every 10 base pairs along the molecule.

sourceRefs.quote: The helix completes a full turn every 34 angstroms, or every 10 base pairs, and the width of the double helix is always 20 angstroms.

equations.expression: Pyrophosphate hydrolysis Delta G degrees approx -7 kcal/mol; cellular Delta G approx -12 kcal/mol
equations.meaning: Hydrolysis of linked phosphate anhydrides is strongly favorable and even more favorable under cellular conditions.

sourceRefs.quote: The Delta G degrees for pyrophosphate hydrolysis is about -7 kcal/mol, and the actual cellular Delta G is about -12 kcal/mol.

workedExamples.question: Which strand is complementary to 5-prime-ATGTAT-3-prime?
steps: Use A-T and G-C base pairing.
The complementary sequence written 5-prime to 3-prime is ATACAT.

sourceRefs.quote: Two DNA strands are complementary if their bases can hydrogen bond when the strands are oriented antiparallel.

workedExamples.question: Why is RNA less stable than DNA?
steps: RNA contains ribose, which has a 2-prime hydroxyl.
The 2-prime hydroxyl can attack the backbone phosphate.
That attack can hydrolyze the RNA chain; DNA lacks this 2-prime hydroxyl.
workedExamples.answer: RNA's 2-prime hydroxyl can attack the backbone phosphate and promote hydrolysis; DNA lacks that group.

sourceRefs.quote: The 2-prime hydroxyl in RNA makes RNA less stable because it can nucleophilically attack the backbone phosphate group, causing hydrolysis when the remainder of the chain acts as a leaving group.

checks.question: Which carbon of the sugar ring is joined to the phosphate units in a nucleotide?
checks.explanation: The nucleotide description places the base on carbon 1 and phosphate units on carbon 5.

sourceRefs.quote: Each nucleotide contains a ribose or deoxyribose sugar, a purine or pyrimidine base joined to carbon 1 of the sugar ring, and one, two, or three phosphate units joined to carbon 5 of the sugar ring.

checks.question: True or false: GC-rich DNA generally has a lower Tm than AT-rich DNA.
checks.explanation: GC pairs have three hydrogen bonds while AT pairs have two, so AT-rich DNA has the lower Tm.

sourceRefs.quote: AT-rich DNA has a lower Tm than GC-rich DNA because AT pairs have two hydrogen bonds while GC pairs have three.

checks.question: What chromosomal region is the spindle-fiber attachment site during cell division?
checks.explanation: The centromere is where spindle fibers attach through kinetochores.

sourceRefs.quote: A centromere is the chromosome region where spindle fibers attach during cell division through kinetochores.

practiceQuestions.question: Which choice correctly identifies the DNA purines?
practiceQuestions.explanation: Adenine and guanine are purines with double-ring structures; cytosine and thymine are pyrimidines.

sourceRefs.quote: Adenine and guanine are purines with a double-ring structure.

practiceQuestions.question: Which RNA type carries amino acids to the ribosome?
practiceQuestions.explanation: Transfer RNA carries amino acids from the cytoplasm to the ribosome and helps translate the genetic code.

sourceRefs.quote: Transfer RNA carries amino acids from the cytoplasm to the ribosome and helps translate the genetic code.
Nucleotides are the building blocks of RNA and DNA.
